reason: MATHEMATICALLY INCORRECT FORMULA IN REMARK (lines 416-419): The remark claims that in the 3+1 approach, one uses the chain rule identity (partial/partial x^i) delta^{(3)} = -(partial/partial gamma^i) delta^{(3)} = -(1/gamma-dot^i)(partial/partial t) delta^{(3)}. The FIRST equality is correct: since delta^{(3)}(x - gamma(t)) depends on x^i and gamma^i(t) only through the combination x^i - gamma^i(t), we have (partial/partial x^i) = -(partial/partial gamma^i). However, the SECOND equality is WRONG. The step from (partial/partial gamma^i) to (1/gamma-dot^i)(d/dt) attempts to use the inverse function theorem: since gamma^i = gamma^i(t), we have d/dt = gamma-dot^i * (partial/partial gamma^i), hence (partial/partial gamma^i) = (1/gamma-dot^i)(d/dt). This is invalid for THREE reasons: (1) gamma-dot^i can be zero (e.g., turning points in spatial coordinates), making 1/gamma-dot^i undefined. (2) Even when gamma-dot^i \!= 0, the map t -> gamma^i(t) is generally not injective (a particle can return to the same spatial coordinate), so the local inverse is not well-defined globally. (3) The operator d/dt acts on ALL components of gamma simultaneously; writing (partial/partial gamma^i) = (1/gamma-dot^i)(d/dt) incorrectly treats gamma^i as the sole t-dependent quantity, ignoring that delta^{(3)} depends on gamma^1, gamma^2, gamma^3, all of which are functions of t. The correct 3+1 calculation uses the identity (d/dt)[delta^{(3)}(x-gamma(t))] = -gamma-dot^i (partial/partial x^i) delta^{(3)}(x-gamma(t)) (sum over i), which is a proper multivariable chain rule. The formula as written in the remark is mathematically incorrect, not merely 'formal' -- it gives the wrong answer when applied literally.
